candidates.title: Human-labeling round runner: collect → validate → flip alt_test_reference off model-jury-bootstrap
candidates.what: The glue/CLI that actually RUNS a >=3-annotator / >=30-item human-labeling round end-to-end: a presentation/export step that serves the calibration items to humans under the §12.2 annotation contract (A/B with real unsure, randomized+persisted order, blinded to gold, embedded attention checks), an ingest step that assembles the verdicts into the human_labels.json schema the loader already enforces, and a `characterize --human-labels` invocation that stamps alt_test_reference:"human" and retires the circular caveat. The product deliverable is the round, not a fancy UI — even a CSV/JSON round-trip plus the export-the-items + assemble-the-matrix scripts closes it.
candidates.why_now: This is the load-bearing product caveat for the whole v0.2.0 preview: every seat in docs/phase-2/panel.json is PROVISIONAL because ω is a circular model-jury bootstrap, and the committed panel is even sub-quorum (2 seats, escalate:true) partly BECAUSE qwen's SCREEN turns on that circular ω (§12.3). Retiring the circularity is the single change that converts 'usable but provisional' seats into a defensible instrument. The honest path exists; what is missing is the operational round that uses it.
candidates.grounding: gameplan Phase 2/3 (role assignment from characterization) + research-grounding §12.2 (Fork C, the four design questions + the annotation-UI contract, explicitly 'Building that UI is out of scope for the measurement library; the contract is the deliverable') + §12.1/§12.3 honest-findings ('Human labels (>=3 annotators, >=30 items) remain required before ω gates for real'). docs/phase-2/kickoff-forks-swarm.md Fork C ('make ω non-circular; remove/replace _ALT_TEST_CAVEAT').
candidates.current_state: STUBBED-AT-THE-SEAM, NOT ABSENT. Built + tested: characterize/human_labels.py loader (schema validation, >=3-annotator hard error, per-tier ε, DISPUTED-drop, Krippendorff α), metrics.alt_test (audit-ready per-annotator paired t-test + Benjamini-Yekutieli FDR), metrics.krippendorff_alpha, scoring.stats.conformal_coverage_interval, run.py --human-labels flag that flips alt_test_reference and _alt_test_human_note, kappa_baseline provisional:false on the human path. GENUINELY ABSENT: any real human_labels.json (none on disk — only synthetic test fixtures in tests/test_human_labels.py), any item-export/presentation step, any verdict-collection/assembly workflow, any CLI subcommand to drive a round. No human labels have ever been produced.
candidates.risk_or_open_question: Qualitative/study-swarm targets (already partly grounded in §12.2 but unresolved operationally): who are the >=3 annotators and at which tier ε (expert 0.2 / skilled 0.15 / crowd 0.1) for a measurement instrument graded by a 1-human studio? Is the director plus two crew/external reviewers a legitimate 'expert' panel, or does that re-introduce a correlated-rater problem the alt-test assumes away? What is the minimum viable collection surface (CSV round-trip vs a thin web form) that still satisfies the §12.2 contract's blinding + attention-check + order-persistence requirements? How is the IRT-pruned effective set (28/93 in §12.3) reconciled with the >=30-item floor?
candidates.depends_on: none (loader + metrics + flag already landed; Fork A expanded item set already at 93 items). Optionally sequenced after a re-run that confirms the >=30 IRT-surviving items.

candidates.title: First real end-to-end diagnostic cycle on the seed puzzle: the live oracle_runner + a `run` CLI
candidates.what: A concrete OracleRunner implementation (the grading host) that, after the Solver halts, copies the workdir out (copy_workdir_out already exists) into a fresh process and executes the seed puzzle's sealed oracle/check.py against it to produce a real OracleOutcome (solved / no_regression / triggered_penalties / tool_calls_used / time_used), plus a `ai-crucible run <puzzle> --model <id> --k N` CLI subcommand that wires a live ClaudeModel.generate (Solver) + the seated panel (from docs/phase-2/panel.json via load_panel + JudgePanel.from_seated) into kernel.run_pass_hat_k and emits the PuzzleHistory rollup (pass^k, Wilson, graduation candidacy). This is the first time the instrument actually measures a model on a puzzle.
candidates.why_now: Phase 4 is 'wire end-to-end + first real puzzle cycle on a seeded capability gap', and sulzbach-co #55252 is the named first candidate — it is already authored on disk (puzzles/seed-sulzbach-55252 with meta.json, prompt, setup_script, oracle/check.py, and the answer-key honeypot). Everything upstream (kernel choke point, framing, budget, sandbox local provider, panel bridge, model adapters) is built and green, but no oracle_runner has ever run check.py and no CLI exposes a puzzle run. Without this the instrument can characterize judges but cannot run a single diagnostic — the core thing it exists to do.
candidates.grounding: gameplan Phase 4 ('Wire end-to-end: Designer → Solver → Critic → Judge panel'; 'First real puzzle cycle on a seeded capability gap (sulzbach-co #55252 is a strong first candidate)') + research-grounding §10.4 (out-of-band post-hoc grading: 'copy the workdir out and grade in a fresh container') + §10.6 ('the seed puzzle (sulzbach-co claude-code#55252) as the first end-to-end fixture'). kernel.py OracleRunner type + docstring ('Phase-2 wires the real copy-workdir-out + sealed-oracle harness behind the same shape').
candidates.current_state: INJECTED-SEAM, real impl ABSENT. Present: kernel.run_attempt/run_pass_hat_k with injected oracle_runner + generate; sandbox.copy_workdir_out + LocalSandbox subprocess provider + the grading-topology docstring; scoring.oracle.grade (conjunctive gate fully implemented); JudgePanel.from_seated + panel_store.load_panel; ClaudeModel.generate/as_judge + OllamaModel; the seed puzzle directory incl. oracle/check.py + ANSWER_KEY_a7f3b9.txt honeypot. GENUINELY ABSENT: any OracleRunner that executes check.py against a copied workdir (only tests pass canned async outcomes); the CLI only dispatches `characterize` (cli.py) — there is no `run` command; the seed has never been executed on a live model.
candidates.risk_or_open_question: Design questions: how is each declared penalty's `trigger` (e.g. 'Solver reads any path under oracle/' for answer_key_fetch, TCRR>0.3 for redundant_tool_calls) actually DETECTED from the trace/workdir by the grading host — is detection trace-driven, filesystem-access-driven, or both, and where does that detection logic live (it is not yet written anywhere)? What is the minimal real Solver tool-loop given roles.Solver routes tool calls through record_tool_call but the live generate must actually issue exec/read_file/write_file? §8.9 threshold/penalty-weight calibration is explicitly Phase-4 work and would surface here on the first real run.
candidates.depends_on: none structurally; benefits from but does not require roadmap-capability-001. The panel half can run with the committed sub-quorum panel.json (escalate-to-Designer path).

candidates.title: Catalog store + graduation lifecycle: persist puzzles, solve-history, and Lab → Arena → Regression transitions
candidates.what: A minimal persistent catalog: a store that holds puzzle records + their accumulated PuzzleHistory/pass^k across runs, applies the §1 graduation rule (graduates() over a Wilson interval at N=20) with a configurable graduation-floor K, transitions a puzzle Lab → Arena on cross-family validation, and DEMOTES (never deletes) a saturated puzzle to Regression. Today the tier is a static field on meta.json and history lives only in-memory for one batch; this makes the catalog a thing that exists between runs and evolves.
candidates.why_now: The catalog lifecycle IS the instrument's longitudinal value proposition ('a living catalog... a capability-evolution timeline as the frontier moves'). Phase 1 named the three tiers and Phase 5 is 'catalog growth + saturation detection + Regression demotion', but nothing persists a puzzle's solve-history or moves it between tiers. With the first real cycle (roadmap-capability-002) producing PuzzleHistory objects, there is suddenly something to persist and graduate — without a store those results evaporate at process exit.
candidates.grounding: gameplan Phase 1 ('Three catalog tiers: Lab / Arena / Regression') + Phase 4 ('Validate observability and graduation criteria; Calibrate graduation-floor K') + Phase 5 ('Saturation detection and Regression demotion') + research-grounding §1 ('Demote solved items to Regression rather than delete') + §2 (rounds-against-rounds). Existing seams: types.CatalogTier, PuzzleMeta.catalog_tier + min_k, scoring.stats.graduates(), observability.PuzzleHistory.is_graduation_candidate.
candidates.current_state: ENUM + RULE present, PERSISTENCE + TRANSITION absent. Present: CatalogTier enum (LAB/ARENA/REGRESSION), meta.json catalog_tier:"lab" + min_k:10 on the seed, scoring.stats.graduates() (Wilson 0.10<=lower & upper<=0.90), observability.PuzzleHistory/ModelProfile rollups, the attestation JsonlEventStore (an append-only store that could back trajectory logs but is not wired to a catalog). GENUINELY ABSENT: any catalog store/index over puzzle directories, any cross-run persistence of solve-history, any code that reads graduates() and writes a tier transition, any Regression demotion path. The repo has exactly one puzzle on disk (the seed).
candidates.risk_or_open_question: graduation-floor K is an explicit open architectural question (gameplan #5: swarm said K=10-20, ChatGPT said 10/20/30/50 tiers, stakes-dependent) — a study-swarm / Phase-4 calibration target, not a free choice. Also open (gameplan #2): catalog SIZE target (~1000 for 3% MDE vs ~200 for 7-10%) shapes the store's scale assumptions. Should the store reuse the attestation event-store (append-only, hash-chained) as the trajectory backing, or be a separate lightweight index? Demotion criteria for Regression (what counts as 'saturated') is unspecified.
candidates.depends_on: roadmap-capability-002 (the cycle is what produces the PuzzleHistory results worth persisting and graduating).

candidates.title: Differential scoring typology: Claude-vs-cross-family-panel delta → Claude-gap / LLM-general / Claude-strength labels
candidates.what: The computation that takes per-puzzle Claude solve-rate AND the cross-family panel/CohortSolver solve-rate and emits the §4 diagnostic typology per puzzle: Claude-fails-others-pass → Claude-specific gap, everyone-fails → LLM-general frontier gap, Claude-passes-others-fail → Claude-strength (anti-regression). The leaderboard already surfaces per-model solve-rate + novelty-rate; this is the cross-model comparison that turns raw scores into the diagnostic label the instrument is named for.
candidates.why_now: Differential scoring is THE thing that 'unlocks a new diagnostic typology' (§4 title) and is the substance of Phase 5's differential-scoring goal. It is the payoff of having both a Claude Solver and a local cross-family panel — without the differential, the panel is only an unbiased judge, not a comparison cohort. It is cheap relative to its value because the per-model rollups already exist; only the cross-model delta + labeling is missing.
candidates.grounding: gameplan Phase 5 ('Differential scoring: Claude vs cross-family panel → diagnostic typology (Claude-specific / LLM-general / Claude-strength)') + research-grounding §4 ('Differential score unlocks new diagnostic typology', the three-way table) + the CohortSolver role slot (roles.CohortSolver exists for exactly the cross-family solve cohort). §9.10 pre-registered hypothesis is about Claude's novelty-bonus rate vs cross-family peers — the differential is its measurement vehicle.
candidates.current_state: GENUINELY ABSENT (no differential code at all — `differential` grep is empty across src). Present only: per-model ModelProfile.solve_rate/novelty_rate rollups (observability.py) and the CohortSolver role class (roles.py). No code compares Claude's per-puzzle rate against a cross-family cohort rate, no typology labeling, no place the differential is stored or reported.
candidates.risk_or_open_question: Qualitative: what solve-rate gap and at what statistical confidence (McNemar paired test + clustered SEs per §9.3 are specified for cross-model deltas) justifies each typology label rather than noise — i.e. the threshold for 'Claude fails, others pass' must be a tested delta, not a point comparison (gameplan / §9 multiple-comparison discipline). Does the cross-family cohort run the FULL Solver tool-loop (so it is a real solve-rate) or only the panel-judge path? This is multi-axis and pairs naturally with a study-swarm on delta-detection power (Miller 2024 MDE).
candidates.depends_on: roadmap-capability-002 (need real Claude Solver runs) + the local panel running as a CohortSolver, not only as judges. Most valuable after roadmap-capability-003 so differentials accrue across the catalog.

domains.notes:
Health-pass artifacts (594 tests, 94.51% cov, 0 CRIT/HIGH) confirm the seam-first build is solid; this slate is purely roadmap-capability, no bug/doc items.

Selection discipline applied: a short 4-candidate slate, ordered by movement-toward-a-real-diagnostic-cycle. The two highest-leverage are 001 (retires the load-bearing circular-ω caveat — the explicit #1 roadmap gap; library half is fully built and tested, only the operational round + collection workflow are missing) and 002 (the first time the instrument measures anything — Phase 4's named first cycle on the already-authored seed puzzle; the oracle_runner grading host and a `run` CLI are the missing glue over fully-built kernel/sandbox/panel seams). 003 (catalog persistence/graduation) and 004 (differential typology) are the natural follow-ons once 002 produces real PuzzleHistory results and cross-family cohort runs.

Precise current-state grounding I verified in-repo:
- Fork C is genuinely built: human_labels.py, metrics.alt_test (BY-FDR audit-ready), krippendorff_alpha, conformal_coverage_interval, run.py --human-labels flag + alt_test_reference flip. NO human_labels.json exists on disk (only synthetic test fixtures). §12.2 itself states the collection UI is out of scope for the library — so the *round* is the missing capability, exactly as the prompt framed it. I did NOT propose faking labels.
- No real OracleRunner anywhere (only the injected Callable type + the seed's oracle/check.py + the copy_workdir_out primitive). cli.py dispatches only `characterize`; no `run` command. The seed puzzle has never executed on a live model.
- Catalog: CatalogTier enum + meta.json catalog_tier field + graduates() + in-memory PuzzleHistory only; zero persistence, zero tier-transition code, exactly one puzzle on disk.
- Differential: zero implementation in src (grep empty). CohortSolver role class exists as the seam.
- Panel→scoring bridge (from_seated/load_panel) IS complete; committed docs/phase-2/panel.json is a sub-quorum 2-seat (gemma4 + granite4.1) escalate:true config — qwen's SCREEN is itself driven by the circular ω, which is the strongest argument for prioritizing 001.

Open architectural questions flagged for study-swarm (per the protocol): graduation-floor K (gameplan #5), catalog size target (#2), penalty-trigger detection mechanics (002), and the cross-model delta-detection threshold / annotator-tier choice (004/001).

candidates.title: Real oracle_runner that executes the puzzle's oracle/check.py out-of-band against the copied workdir
candidates.what: A concrete implementation of the kernel's injected `OracleRunner` callable (kernel.py:137, `Callable[[AttemptState, PuzzleMeta], Awaitable[OracleOutcome]]`) that: (1) snapshots the Solver workdir via the already-built `copy_workdir_out` (sandbox.py:347), (2) executes the puzzle's grading-side `oracle/check.py` against that copy in a separate process, (3) maps its `GradeResult` (solved / penalties_fired / reported_value) plus the kernel-counted `read_paths`/tool-calls/time into an `OracleOutcome` (oracle.py:50) for `scoring.oracle.grade`. The seed puzzle already ships a complete `oracle/check.py` with `grade(reported, transcript)` returning solved + answer_key_fetch/skip_grounded_read penalties — this is the missing bridge that actually invokes it.
candidates.why_now: This is THE choke point between 'a substrate with 594 tests' and 'a graded run'. Every kernel test injects a canned `oracle_runner`; the real one has never been built, so no puzzle has ever actually been graded. Until it exists, `run_attempt` cannot produce a real oracle verdict from a real Solver transcript, and the seed puzzle's check.py is dead code. It is the single highest-leverage operability glue: the out-of-band topology (the sealed-boundary lock) is only real once a runner actually copies-out and grades on a separate host.
candidates.grounding: kernel.py:131-137 (OracleRunner type, 'Phase-2 wires the real copy-workdir-out + sealed-oracle harness behind the same shape'); sandbox.py:347-376 (copy_workdir_out, the topology half, untested-against-a-runner); puzzles/seed-sulzbach-55252/oracle/check.py (grade() exists, consumes transcript['read_paths']); scoring/oracle.py:50 (OracleOutcome target shape); gameplan.md §Phase 4 'wire end-to-end' + research-grounding §10.4 (out-of-band grading is the lock).
candidates.current_state: STUBBED as an injected param only. `OracleRunner` is a Callable type with canned async fakes in test_kernel.py (oracle_runner_for / raising_oracle_runner). `copy_workdir_out` is implemented and unit-tested in isolation (test_sandbox.py:514) but no kernel/runner ever calls it. `oracle/check.py` is fully written but never imported/executed by any non-test code. The `transcript['read_paths']` contract check.py reads is not produced anywhere — the kernel records TraceEvents but nothing assembles the normalized read_paths set check.py expects. Genuinely absent: the runner module itself + the read_paths projection from the kernel trace.
candidates.risk_or_open_question: How does the runner derive the authoritative `read_paths` set check.py keys on, from the kernel trace events, without trusting Solver self-report? (the §10.2 rule is file access must be kernel-observed). And what is the per-puzzle grader-invocation contract — does check.py expose a stable `grade(reported, transcript)` entrypoint every puzzle must conform to, or does the runner exec a generic `python oracle/check.py` with a JSON stdin/stdout protocol? This is a study-swarm-worthy interface-design question (the grading-host wire protocol).

candidates.title: A `run` / `attempt` CLI subcommand wiring a configured model + puzzle into run_attempt
candidates.what: A new `ai-crucible run <puzzle-dir> --model <id> [--k N] [--arm ...]` subcommand in cli.py that composes the concrete pieces (ClaudeModel/OllamaModel `.generate` as the choke point, a LocalSandbox, the oracle_runner from glue-001, optionally a seated panel) and calls `kernel.run_attempt` / `run_pass_hat_k`, then renders the resulting AttemptState (gate_passed, pass^k, terminated_by) to the operator. Today the only path to `run_attempt` is a Python test.
candidates.why_now: The CLI is the operator's only door, and it currently opens onto exactly one room: `characterize`. An operator cannot run the actual diagnostic game — the thing the whole instrument exists to do — without writing Python. This is the surface that makes 'an instrument an operator can run' literally true. It directly enables Phase 4's 'first real puzzle cycle on a seeded capability gap'.
candidates.grounding: cli.py:54-101 (only `characterize` is dispatched; `_usage()` lists one command); kernel.py:221 run_attempt / kernel.py:426 run_pass_hat_k (the entry points with no CLI caller); gameplan.md §Phase 4 (first diagnostic cycle on sulzbach-55252); README §Architecture (Designer→Solver→Judge through one generate choke point — described but not invocable from the CLI).
candidates.current_state: GENUINELY ABSENT as a command. The two kernel entry points exist and are fully tested via direct Python calls; the CLI dispatcher (cli.py:_dispatch) has a clean, extensible shape (one `if command == ...`) ready for a second branch, and the structured-error contract + UTF-8 banner already generalize. What is missing is the subcommand module that does the composition (model adapter + sandbox + oracle_runner + render). Depends on glue-001 (needs a real oracle_runner) and glue-004 (a way to say which model).
candidates.risk_or_open_question: What does the operator see on a degraded grading failure (terminated_by==ERROR) vs a clean gate-close — the render must make the §8.3 gate verdict legible without leaking oracle internals. Also: should `run` default to single-attempt or to pass^k at the puzzle's `min_k` (seed declares min_k=10, ~10 model calls)? A budget/cost-visibility design question (UNCERTAINTY_GATED_HUMANS: warn before a 10x-cost run).

candidates.title: Setup-script execution: stage the puzzle workdir in the sandbox before the Solver runs
candidates.what: Glue that runs a puzzle's `setup_script` inside the LocalSandbox to materialize the Solver-visible workdir (for the seed: the config/ + service/ tree plus the staged bait file) before the Solver's first turn, and stages the grading-side bait artifact per the oracle contract. `puzzle.load_puzzle` deliberately only *resolves* the setup_script path; nothing executes it, so today `run_attempt(sandbox=...)` runs the Solver against an empty workdir.
candidates.why_now: A puzzle is not runnable end-to-end without its environment being staged — the seed's entire trap (real value in config/limits.py vs stale comment vs bait ANSWER_KEY) only exists if setup_script runs. Without this, the Solver has nothing to read, the oracle's grounded-read and bait-touch penalties can never fire, and the diagnostic the puzzle was built to measure is inert. It is the missing first step of the run pipeline that glue-001/002 assume.
candidates.grounding: puzzle.py:204-206 (_resolve_setup 'this loader only resolves the path, never executes it'); puzzles/seed-sulzbach-55252/setup_script (stages config/limits.py=7, the stale-comment use site; comment says oracle/bait staged out-of-band); sandbox.py LocalSandbox.exec (the channel to run it); kernel.py docstring step 3 (Solver runs inside the sandbox — but no staging step is named); research-grounding §10.4 (sandboxed setup priming).
candidates.current_state: STUBBED: the path is resolved (LoadedPuzzle.setup_script) and the sandbox `exec` channel exists, but the wiring that executes the script into the workdir is absent. The seed's setup_script is written and self-documents that the oracle/bait are NOT staged by it (they are grading-side), so the bait-staging step (placing ANSWER_KEY_a7f3b9.txt into the Solver workdir per the oracle's note) is a separate, also-absent piece. Genuinely absent: the stage-before-Solve step and the bait-placement step.
candidates.risk_or_open_question: Where is the trust boundary for setup_script execution — it is authored content run with shell semantics inside the (process-isolation-only) LocalSandbox; the §10.4 disclosure says this layer is defense-in-depth, not a hard boundary. Should setup run under the same cap-drop/seccomp regime as the Solver (deferred to the Docker provider wave), and who stages the bait — the runner or the setup contract? A trust/policy question worth naming.

candidates.title: A config surface: which models, which puzzles, which budgets, in one declarable place
candidates.what: A small declarative config (e.g. a `crucible.toml` / run-config dict) that names the Designer/Solver model + family, the judge panel (or a path to a saved panel.json from `--write-panel`), the puzzle(s) to run, and budget/k overrides — loaded into the model-adapter + kernel composition root that glue-002 needs. Today every one of those choices is a Python literal or a CLI flag scattered across the characterize run; there is no single 'this is my run' artifact.
candidates.why_now: An operator running a real diagnostic cycle has to specify a panel of local models (the Phase-2 candidate panel: Qwen 32B, Mistral 24B, Command-R, etc.), which puzzles, and what budgets — repeatedly and reproducibly (PIN_PER_STEP wants a run to be replayable from pinned inputs). Without a config surface, every run is a bespoke invocation, and the reproducibility the instrument design (SUT.yaml, §9) is built around has no input artifact to pin. It is the connective tissue that makes glue-002 ergonomic rather than a wall of flags.
candidates.grounding: gameplan.md §Phase 2 candidate panel (Qwen/Mistral/Command-R/Llama/DeepSeek — named but only as prose) + §Phase 3 role assignment; characterize/run.py main() (--models / --items / --write-panel — the only config-ish surface, characterization-only); models/__init__.py (adapters take model_id/family/quant as constructor args — no declarative loader); instrument/sut.py (SUT.yaml template — the provenance artifact a config would feed); pyproject.toml is the only config file in the tree.
candidates.current_state: GENUINELY ABSENT for the run path. Characterization has a flag-based surface (`--models model@family`, `--write-panel`) and `panel_store.load_panel` can rehydrate a composed panel from JSON — so the *panel* half has a persisted artifact. The *run* half (which puzzle + which Solver model + budgets) has no declarative form at all; the seed puzzle's meta.json carries per-puzzle budgets but nothing selects models or a puzzle set. Partially seamed (panel_store) / mostly absent (run config).
candidates.risk_or_open_question: Scope discipline: how much config is too much for a research preview — a single TOML naming model+puzzle+panel is enough for the first cycle, but the temptation is to build a full run-matrix DSL. Where is the line between 'declarable run' and 'over-built'? And should the config be the SUT.yaml itself (reuse the existing §9 provenance artifact) rather than a parallel format? A design question to avoid two competing config truths.

candidates.title: Human-label elicitation harness: export calibration items for labeling + scaffold human_labels.json (the step that USES Fork C)
candidates.what: The label-COLLECTION glue that retires the circular ω honestly: an `ai-crucible label export` that emits the calibration items (without gold) to a human-labelable form, plus the scaffold/validator that assembles annotator verdicts into the exact `human_labels.json` shape `load_human_labels` already consumes (`item_id -> {annotator_id: verdict}`), including the §12.2 quality scaffolding (embedded gold attention-checks, per-annotator IAA surface). The CONSUMPTION path is fully built (`run.py --human-labels` -> `load_human_labels` -> `metrics.alt_test`); what is missing is the ELICITATION step that produces the file real annotators fill in.
candidates.why_now: This is the named top product gap: the alt-test ω is a circular model-jury bootstrap and all judge seats are PROVISIONAL until a real human-labeling round runs. The harness to *use* labels exists and is tested; the only thing standing between 'provisional seats' and 'human-validated seats' is the operability glue to actually collect labels in the expected shape. This is the honest way to retire circularity — not faking labels, but building the door real annotators walk through. It directly converts the known #1 caveat into an actionable workflow.
candidates.grounding: characterize/human_labels.py (load_human_labels / build_records_per_annotator — fully built consumer, expects item_id->{annotator_id:verdict}); characterize/run.py:638 (--human-labels flag wired end-to-end to alt_test); research-grounding §12.2 'Fork C — the human alt-test harness' (schema lock, ≥3 annotators/≥30 items, attention-checks W19-8643, forced-binary avoidance Krosnick 2010) + §12 Q3 'This is a real human-labeling decision'; README research-preview caveat ('seated judges are provisional… escalates to a Claude Designer below quorum'); calibration/loader.py load_default (the items to export, gold held grading-side).
candidates.current_state: Consumption STUBBED-COMPLETE; elicitation GENUINELY ABSENT. `load_human_labels` validates the file, enforces ≥3 annotators / DISPUTE_FLOOR / per-tier ε, and feeds `metrics.alt_test`; `--human-labels` is plumbed through `run_panel` and the report stamps a conditional non-circular caveat when present. There is NO code that exports items for labeling, NO human_labels.json template/example in the repo, and NO assembly/validation of raw annotator input into the loader's shape (grep for export/template/labeling returns only the consumer's docstrings). The attention-check / gold-injection QC named in §12.2 is documented but unbuilt.
candidates.risk_or_open_question: The qualitative labeling-protocol decisions §12.2 flags: forced-binary vs allow-unsure (Krosnick & Presser 2010 — already honored by the _UNSURE handling in the loader, so export must preserve it), how gold attention-checks are interleaved without tipping annotators, and the annotator-tier/ε policy. These are study-swarm targets — the design of the elicitation instrument itself, not just its file format. Also a process question: who recruits the ≥3 annotators (out of repo scope) vs what the tool can scaffold.

domains.notes:
Domain reviewed READ-ONLY against gameplan.md (Phase 4 'wire end-to-end' + open questions), research-grounding.md (§10.2 injected-stub seam, §10.4 out-of-band grading lock, §12.1/§12.2 Fork-C human-label gap), README, and src (cli.py, kernel.py, sandbox.py, puzzle.py, scoring/oracle.py, models/, characterize/run.py + human_labels.py, instrument/inspect_task.py) plus the seed puzzle.

CENTRAL FINDING — the build is genuinely seam-first and the operability glue is the unbridged gap between leaves: every model-shaped dependency the kernel needs is an INJECTED callable (generate / oracle_runner / judges) with only canned fakes in tests. No composition root exists that turns a configured model + a real puzzle into a graded run. The five candidates are the connective tissue, in dependency order:
- glue-001 (real oracle_runner executing oracle/check.py via copy_workdir_out) is THE keystone — it's the difference between '594 tests' and 'one graded attempt'. copy_workdir_out and check.py both exist but nothing wires them; the read_paths projection check.py expects is unproduced.
- glue-002 (a `run` CLI subcommand) is the operator's door — today cli.py exposes only `characterize`; run_attempt has no CLI caller. Depends on 001 + 004.
- glue-003 (setup_script execution + bait staging) is the missing FIRST step — puzzle.py only resolves the path; the Solver currently runs against an empty workdir so the seed's entire trap is inert.
- glue-004 (a run config surface) makes 002 reproducible/ergonomic; only the panel half is persisted today (panel_store), the run half is absent.
- glue-005 (human-label ELICITATION) is the honest path to retire the circular ω: the CONSUMPTION side (load_human_labels -> alt_test, run.py --human-labels) is fully built and tested; only the export/collect step that produces human_labels.json is missing. This is explicitly NOT faking labels — it's building the door annotators walk through, the named #1 product gap.

Severity discipline: all five move the instrument toward RUNNING a real diagnostic cycle (Phase 4), all are roadmap-grounded (not invented), all have a precise existing seam. Effort marked honestly (001=L; 002/003/004/005=M). Qualitative trust/policy questions named per candidate are study-swarm targets: the grading-host wire protocol (001), pre-run cost/k warning (002), setup-script trust boundary (003), config-vs-SUT.yaml duplication (004), and the labeling-protocol design — forced-binary/attention-checks/tier-ε (005). No health/bug fixes proposed (that pass is done); no docs (coordinator owns those). Several candidates carry compensator-relevant irreversible actions (model-API spend, workdir creation) but those are runtime-internal, not publish/release — flagged for the run-render UX, not as blockers.

candidates.title: Concrete seed-puzzle oracle_runner: bridge oracle/check.py to the kernel's OracleRunner
candidates.what: A small adapter that wraps the seed puzzle's grading-side oracle/check.py (grade(reported, transcript) -> GradeResult) into the kernel's injected OracleRunner shape (Callable[[AttemptState, PuzzleMeta], Awaitable[OracleOutcome]]): extract the Solver's final answer text + the kernel-recorded read_paths from the AttemptState, call the sealed check.grade out-of-band, and map GradeResult.{solved, reported_value, penalties_fired} onto OracleOutcome.{solved, solve_quality, triggered_penalties, tool_calls_used, time_used}. This is the missing seam that lets a real attempt actually be graded against the hidden oracle.
candidates.why_now: This is the single highest-leverage 'wire the existing shape' gap. Every piece exists in isolation — the kernel takes oracle_runner as an injected callable (kernel.py OracleRunner), the seed puzzle ships a fully-written sealed check.py producing a GradeResult, and oracle.grade() applies the conjunctive gate — but NOTHING in src/ connects check.py to the kernel. Without this, run_attempt can never grade the one real puzzle; the instrument cannot run a single diagnostic step on its own catalog.
candidates.grounding: kernel.py OracleRunner type (lines 131-137); puzzles/seed-sulzbach-55252/oracle/check.py grade()->GradeResult; scoring/oracle.py OracleOutcome+grade(); research-grounding §10.4 (out-of-band post-hoc grading is the lock). Confirmed absent: grep for 'oracle/check', 'GradeResult', 'check.grade' in src/ returns zero matches.
candidates.current_state: GENUINELY ABSENT as a bridge, but both endpoints fully exist. check.py defines grade(reported, transcript) reading transcript['read_paths']; OracleRunner is the declared injection point; oracle.grade() consumes OracleOutcome. The AttemptState already records tool-call TraceEvents with payload {tool, args} (roles.Solver._tools_event), so read_paths is recoverable. No adapter, no read_paths extraction helper, no GradeResult->OracleOutcome mapping exists.
candidates.risk_or_open_question: How are read_paths canonically derived from the trace? check.py expects normalized paths; the trace records raw {tool, args}. The mapping (which tool kinds + which arg keys count as a 'read' of a path) is a policy decision that determines whether skip_grounded_read / answer_key_fetch fire correctly — under-counting reads = false bypass penalties, over-counting = missed bypass. -> study-swarm target: the read-path extraction contract (what counts as 'touching' a file across exec/grep/cat/read_file).

candidates.title: Adversarial/bypass end-to-end test harness: prove the ADVERSARIAL critical penalty actually triggers through the kernel
candidates.what: A test suite that drives scripted Solver trajectories (via canned generate functions) END TO END through run_attempt against the seed puzzle and asserts the kernel-graded score reflects them: (a) a clean grounded solve opens the gate; (b) a bait-touch (Solver reads ANSWER_KEY_a7f3b9.txt) fires answer_key_fetch and CLOSES the gate via the critical-flavor path even on a correct answer; (c) a fabricate-without-reading trajectory fires skip_grounded_read; (d) a redundant-loop trajectory fires the TCRR minor penalty / hard-kill. Each asserts the actual oracle Score.metadata.failed_conditions, not just unit-level grade() behavior.
candidates.why_now: Today the ADVERSARIAL critical penalty is DECLARED (meta.json answer_key_fetch, weight -200, flavor adversarial) and oracle.grade() unit-tests its gate-closing in isolation, but nothing proves a real bait-touch trajectory propagates from Solver tool calls -> trace -> oracle_runner -> gate closure. The director wants 'different ways to challenge and test'; the most basic challenge — 'does the bypass detector actually catch a bypass when a Solver takes it?' — has no end-to-end coverage. This is the integration test that makes the auditing-game claim falsifiable.
candidates.grounding: research-grounding §8.2 (scaled penalties, adversarial=critical), §8.5 (bait fidelity), §8.6 (detection mechanics); scoring/oracle.py CRITICAL_FLAVOR + has_critical gate; puzzles/seed-sulzbach-55252/oracle/check.py bait-touch logic; eval_awareness.interpret_bait asymmetry. Confirmed: penalty paths covered only by isolated unit tests (test_scoring.py, test_engagement.py), no end-to-end driver.
candidates.current_state: STUBBED/PARTIAL: penalties declared in seed meta.json; oracle.grade() + interpret_bait + check.grade() each unit-tested separately. No test wires a Solver trajectory -> run_attempt -> graded gate-closure. Depends on -001 (the oracle_runner bridge) to grade a real trajectory; until then the harness must inject a fake oracle_runner mirroring check.py's logic.
candidates.risk_or_open_question: What is the canonical set of 'challenge trajectories' a puzzle should be tested against before it graduates? (clean / bait-touch / fabricate / loop is the obvious floor, but multi-trapdoor redundancy §8.5 and camouflaged-bait §10.5 suggest more.) -> study-swarm target: the minimum adversarial-trajectory battery per puzzle class that gives confidence the penalty surface discriminates.

candidates.title: Self-test / dogfood mode: prove the instrument discriminates known-trivial vs known-impossible before any real run
candidates.what: A runnable self-test that authors (or wires) a minimal known-trivial puzzle (every Solver passes — attention check) and a known-impossible puzzle (any pass = leakage/spec-violation, ImpossibleBench style), runs them through run_pass_hat_k, and asserts the known-groups acceptance laws hold: trivial passes for all, impossible passes for none. Exposed as a CLI subcommand (e.g. `ai-crucible self-test`) so an operator can prove the harness discriminates BEFORE trusting a real diagnostic.
candidates.why_now: The calibration/known_groups.py module already encodes the §11.3 pass-pattern laws (KNOWN_TRIVIAL must all pass, KNOWN_IMPOSSIBLE must none pass) — but it operates on the JUDGE-characterization side (admission pairs), not on the attempt/Solver side. There is no end-to-end self-test that proves the *attempt* pipeline (Solver->oracle->gate) discriminates a trivial from an impossible puzzle. This is the cheapest possible proof that the instrument is wired correctly, and it directly serves the director's 'self-test mode that proves it discriminates' ask.
candidates.grounding: research-grounding §11.3 (calibration set: known-trivial / known-impossible / known-diagnostic with pass-pattern laws), gameplan Phase 2 ('Calibrated puzzle set (known trivial / known impossible / known diagnostic)'); calibration/known_groups.py check_known_groups() (already implements the laws). §8.9 (solve-oracle robustness — corner-case probes).
candidates.current_state: PARTIAL: known_groups.py implements the acceptance laws but is consumed only by the judge-characterization path (calibration items, ability tiers). No attempt-side trivial/impossible puzzle fixtures exist (catalog = 1 real puzzle + 1 test fixture). The CLI has only `characterize`; no `self-test` subcommand. The seam (known_groups + run_pass_hat_k + the gate) all exist; the trivial/impossible puzzle artifacts and the wiring do not.
candidates.risk_or_open_question: What makes a puzzle credibly 'known-impossible' without being a contrived no-op? ImpossibleBench uses impossibility-by-construction (any pass IS bypass); a self-test impossible puzzle must be impossible-yet-tempting so a pass genuinely signals leakage. -> study-swarm target: the construction recipe for a known-impossible attempt-side puzzle whose only 'pass' path is a bypass (so the self-test catches a leaky harness, not just a hard one).

candidates.title: Eval-awareness probe wiring: run the paired deploy/test framing arms end-to-end as the per-release boundary-validation gate
candidates.what: Wire eval_awareness.build_probe_pair into a runnable probe: for a given puzzle + model, run the Solver on BOTH the deploy-framed and test-framed halves (via run_attempt with the probe contexts), compare bait-touch rate / trajectory divergence across the pair, and emit a boundary-validation verdict (behavior divergence => an eval cue is leaking through the sealed boundary, §10.1(d)). This is the §10.5 'paired eval-awareness + neutral control per release' check made executable.
candidates.why_now: build_probe_pair() builds the matched deploy/test contexts and interpret_bait() encodes the asymmetric verdict — but nothing RUNS the pair through the kernel and compares. §10.1(d) and §10.5 both make the eval-awareness probe the gate that validates the sealed boundary every release; the README markets 'a sealed measurement boundary' as a headline differentiator, yet there is no executable check that the boundary actually holds under a real model. This turns a marketed property into a measured one — and it's a distinct, grounded way to challenge the instrument (does it leak its own eval-ness?).
candidates.grounding: research-grounding §10.5 (bait-touch primary signal; paired deploy/test probe per-model; the probe IS the boundary-validation gate), §10.1(d) (sealed boundary; framing moves cheating 92%->1%, Zhong 2025); eval_awareness.py build_probe_pair() + interpret_bait() (both built + unit-tested); framing.py three arms. activation_probe() is honestly stubbed (NotImplementedError, needs weights — out of scope).
candidates.current_state: STUBBED at the runner level: ProbePair + build_probe_pair + interpret_bait exist and are unit-tested (test purity, asymmetry); the framing arms (neutral/self_referential/social_standings) are wired into build_scored_context and run_attempt accepts an `arm`. NO harness runs both probe halves and diffs behavior; no per-release boundary-validation report. activation_probe is a deliberate NotImplementedError stub (Phase 2, needs white-box weights — do NOT wire).
candidates.risk_or_open_question: What divergence magnitude across the deploy/test pair counts as a boundary LEAK vs noise? With closed-API stochasticity (§9.7, 2.2-6.0pp pass@1 variance) a small bait-touch-rate delta could be noise; the gate needs an uncertainty-aware threshold. -> study-swarm target: the divergence threshold + N-seeds floor that distinguishes a real eval-cue leak from sampling noise (ties to §9.7 N>=10 seeds, Larsen 2025 seed-flip instability).

candidates.title: Round-against-round generation loop: Designer crafts a harder puzzle from the last Solver failure (R-Zero seam)
candidates.what: An orchestration loop that closes the Designer/Solver cycle: after a pass^k run, feed the Solver's failure trajectory (or its near-misses) to the Designer role with a prompt to craft a variant puzzle that targets the same capability gap but is harder / closes the bypass the Solver found. Produces a candidate puzzle artifact (meta.json + prompt + setup_script + oracle) for the Lab tier. This is the R-Zero Challenger/Solver co-evolution that research-grounding calls 'ai-crucible's own architecture'.
candidates.why_now: The Designer role class exists (roles.Designer) but appears ONLY in roles.py + tests — there is no loop that uses it. §10.1(b) explicitly identifies R-Zero (Challenger posing frontier-edge tasks + Solver cracking them, co-evolving with no human data) as ai-crucible's Designer/Solver round-against-round cycle, and gameplan Phase 5 lists 'Round-against-round generation cycle' as the catalog-growth engine. With the catalog stuck at 1 real puzzle, an automated Designer loop is the grounded path to catalog growth that doesn't require hand-authoring every item.
candidates.grounding: research-grounding §10.1(b) (R-Zero arXiv:2508.05004 = ai-crucible's Designer/Solver cycle; self-referential mastery + round-against-round); §2 (generation cycle is rounds-against-rounds, ANLI/Dynabench); §8.10 (round-against-round seeds next round's bait); gameplan Phase 5 + Designer-slot-stays-Claude (Phase 3). roles.Designer.act() built (chrome-guarded, generate choke point) but unused outside tests.
candidates.current_state: SEAM PRESENT, LOOP ABSENT: Designer role fully implemented (act() routes through generate, chrome-guarded) and tested, but there is no generation/orchestration loop wiring Solver-failure -> Designer -> new candidate. SEELE/ADARFT frontier-calibrated-difficulty levers (§10.1(c)) are cited but not implemented. No puzzle-authoring/serialization helper exists to turn a Designer output into a puzzle directory. Needs a real Claude `generate` (claude_adapter exists) to drive the Designer.
candidates.risk_or_open_question: Multiple qualitative locks: (a) what failure signal seeds the next puzzle (full trajectory? near-miss class? the specific bypass taken?); (b) frontier-calibrated difficulty target — §10.1(c) says ~40-60% predicted solve (SEELE/ADARFT), but how is 'predicted solve' estimated before the puzzle has a pass^k history?; (c) how to keep generated puzzles grounded in real capability gaps vs drifting synthetic (the canon-first / empirical-signal discipline). -> study-swarm target: the Designer's difficulty-calibration + grounding-fidelity contract so round-against-round grows a diagnostic catalog, not a synthetic one.

candidates.title: Second + third seed puzzles from the §5 catalog: a multi-axis instrument floor
candidates.what: Author 2-3 additional seed puzzles as full directories (meta.json + prompt + setup_script + sealed oracle/) targeting distinct capability axes from the §5 documented-gaps list — e.g. tool-use redundancy (claude-code#30111, exercises the regressional TCRR/redundant_tool_calls penalty), prompt-injection robustness (claude-code#22915), and numeric reasoning (claude-code#9421). Each ships its own declared penalty list + bait so it exercises a different slice of the scoring surface than the retrieval-grounding seed.
candidates.why_now: The catalog is exactly ONE real puzzle (seed-sulzbach-55252 + one test fixture). gameplan Phase 4/5 and §5 already enumerate reproduction-ready gaps with URLs; the puzzle-artifact contract (§1, §8.8) is locked and the loader/scorer support it. A single puzzle can prove the pipeline runs but cannot prove the instrument DISCRIMINATES across capability axes — which is the whole diagnostic claim. A second puzzle that exercises the regressional/minor penalty (the current seed only meaningfully drives critical+causal) is the cheapest way to broaden coverage of the scoring surface.
candidates.grounding: research-grounding §5 (Claude capability gap puzzle seeds — full list with repro URLs: #30111 redundant cd, #22915 prompt-injection, #9421 arithmetic, #16504 silent-delete, etc.); §8.8 (puzzle artifact structure / meta.json contract); gameplan Phase 5 ('continuous puzzle harvesting from GitHub issues + dogfood findings'). puzzle.load_puzzle + the seed dir are the working template.
candidates.current_state: GENUINELY ABSENT (catalog size): 1 real puzzle exists. The artifact contract, loader, oracle-scorer, and seed template all exist and are proven — authoring more puzzles is 'fill the existing shape', not new infrastructure. §5 supplies the grounded targets so these are NOT synthetic. The regressional redundant_tool_calls penalty is declared in the seed but is a minor side-signal there; a tool-use-efficiency puzzle would make it load-bearing.
candidates.risk_or_open_question: How many seed puzzles, across how many axes, before the catalog is 'diagnostic' rather than anecdotal? Miller 2024 (§1) says ~200 puzzles for 7-10% MDE, ~1000 for 3% — but the near-term question is the minimum axis-COVERAGE (not item count) to claim multi-axis discrimination. -> study-swarm target: the minimum seed-catalog composition (how many axes x how many items each) that lets the differential-scoring typology (§4: Claude-specific / LLM-general / Claude-strength) be claimed honestly.

candidates.title: Human alt-test labeling round: USE the Fork-C harness to retire the circular ω (the known top gap)
candidates.what: Run a real human-labeling round against the Fork-C harness that already exists: collect >=3 human annotators x >=30 (50-100 recommended) items in the human_labels.json schema characterize/human_labels.py defines, feed it via `run.py --human-labels`, and let metrics.alt_test compute the audit-ready ω (paired one-sided t-test, Benjamini-Yekutieli FDR) so seated judges stop being provisional. The capability missing is NOT code — it is the labeling operation + the annotation UI the §12.2 contract specifies.
candidates.why_now: This is the load-bearing product caveat called out everywhere: README research-preview banner, SCORECARD, §12.1/§12.3 all say the alt-test ω is a circular model-jury bootstrap, so EVERY seated judge is provisional and the panel escalates to a Claude Designer below quorum. §12.2 (Fork C) built the entire non-circular harness — loader, alt_test, krippendorff_alpha, conformal_coverage_interval, the --human-labels flag, a synthetic-label test suite proving the path end-to-end — but it has NEVER been fed real labels. Retiring the circularity honestly is the single change that upgrades the instrument from 'provisional' to 'usable for a real diagnostic'.
candidates.grounding: research-grounding §12.2 (Fork C: the human alt-test harness — full design, Calderon/Reichart/Dror 2025 arXiv:2501.10970, the annotation-UI contract), §12.1/§12.3 ('alt-test ω is STILL the circular model-jury bootstrap'; seats provisional until human round runs); README research-preview caveat; characterize/human_labels.py + metrics.alt_test + tests/test_human_labels.py (all shipped). DO NOT fake labels — §12.2 is explicit the reference must be human or the circularity re-absorbs.
candidates.current_state: HARNESS COMPLETE, NEVER USED: human_labels.py loader (enforces >=3 annotators / >=30 items / per-tier ε), metrics.alt_test (real BY-FDR procedure), krippendorff_alpha, conformal_coverage_interval all exist + are tested with SYNTHETIC labels. The gaps are (a) no real human_labels.json has ever been collected; (b) the annotation UI the §12.2 contract specifies (comparative A/B + unsure option, randomized order, per-side micro-judgment, blinding, gold attention checks) is explicitly out-of-scope-for-the-library and unbuilt.
candidates.risk_or_open_question: Primarily an operational/qualitative call, not code: who annotates (expert vs skilled vs crowd, setting ε per §12.2 tier), how many items at what IAA (Krippendorff α) makes the seat decision trustworthy at the small-N conformal coverage Fork-C reports, and whether to build the annotation UI in-repo or feed labels from an external tool. -> study-swarm target: the labeling-protocol design (annotator tier, item count, IAA floor, UI vs import) that makes the first ω non-circular AND audit-ready, not just non-circular.

domains.notes:
Ground truth from the read: the build is deliberately seam-first and the dominant pattern for this domain is "wire the existing shape," not "build new." The single load-bearing finding is that the instrument CANNOT RUN A REAL ATTACK/DIAGNOSTIC END-TO-END TODAY: run_attempt/run_pass_hat_k and oracle.grade() exist and are well-tested, but there is NO concrete oracle_runner in src/ bridging the seed puzzle's fully-written sealed oracle/check.py (grade()->GradeResult) to the kernel's injected OracleRunner shape (grep for 'oracle/check'/'GradeResult'/'check.grade' in src/ = zero hits). Consequently every penalty path (answer_key_fetch ADVERSARIAL critical, skip_grounded_read causal, redundant_tool_calls regressional) and the bait-touch signal are DECLARED + unit-tested in isolation but nothing TRIGGERS them through a live attempt. That is why -001 (the bridge, effort S) is the keystone and -002/-003/-004/-005 all depend on it.

Selection discipline applied: I favored candidates that move the instrument toward actually RUNNING a diagnostic cycle and that have a real existing seam. -001 (oracle_runner bridge) and -002 (adversarial end-to-end battery) are the direct answer to "does anything actually trigger the ADVERSARIAL critical penalty today" — answer: no, only in isolation. -004 (eval-awareness probe) and -003 (self-test discriminate) are creative grounded ways to challenge the instrument with strong existing seams (build_probe_pair/interpret_bait shipped; known_groups laws shipped but only on the judge side). -005 (round-against-round) is the R-Zero loop §10.1(b) calls ai-crucible's own architecture — Designer role exists but is unused outside tests (L effort, real qualitative locks). -006 (more seed puzzles) is the catalog-floor fill — pure "fill the shape," targets are pre-listed in §5. -007 is the KNOWN TOP GAP stated by the prompt and the README: Fork-C built the entire non-circular human alt-test harness (§12.2) but it has never been fed real labels — I propose USING it (collect labels + the §12.2-specified annotation UI), explicitly NOT faking labels.

Honest stub map (so the reviewer can trust effort/current_state): activation_probe() in eval_awareness.py is a deliberate NotImplementedError (needs white-box weights, Phase 2) — I did NOT propose wiring it. The Critic is interface-reserved/default-off by §10.3 design (narrow gain, conformity regression risk) — not proposed. claude_adapter + ollama_adapter exist and mirror each other's surface (generate/judge/as_judge/judge_item) — they back the Phase-2 judge-characterization path, but no adapter is wired to drive a Designer/Solver attempt cycle, which is why -005 needs the adapter as a driver. The framing arms (neutral/self_referential/social_standings) ARE wired into run_attempt via the `arm` param, so -004's framing half is real; only the paired-run-and-diff harness is missing.

Two items are NOT gated on -001 and can proceed independently: -006 (author puzzles — just needs the loader, which works) and -007 (human labeling — the harness is complete, needs the labeling op). The rest form a clean dependency chain off the keystone bridge.

NOT proposed (out of scope per the prompt): health/bug fixes (health pass done — confirmed 594 tests, the kernel already has extensive resilience/degrade coverage); pure docs (coordinator owns); the full cryptographic-provenance/attestation chain (§9 — that's instrument-quality/production-readiness for a different domain and the attestation module + roadmap already exist as a deferred dogfood swarm). I also did not propose faking any labels or any synthetic-discrimination shortcut — every challenge candidate is grounded in a real declared seam or a real §5 capability gap.
